lines.target: And what of tomorrow? How will British and American English develop over the coming quarter-century?
The Gold Rush gave us words like bonanza, panhandler and prospector,
while settlers talked of staking a claim or striking it rich (if things went well, they bit the dust).
Relaxation mainly took the form of gambling, where players had to put up or shut up.
Gamblers would call your bluff if they didn't believe you were holding a good hand, but you could still hit the jackpot.
The railroad stretched further and across the country, and people either got sidetracked or worse, they went off the rails.
The wiser among them preferred to backtrack rather than find themselves in a difficult situation.
All of these words and phrases (and many more) remain part of our everyday speech.
Given the impact of science, technology,
English will continue to eagerly create new words — rather than just borrowing from other languages as it did in the past,
and to become a real second language for the rest of the world.
The reasons for the spread of this new world language — which is no longer culturally based — are debatable.
Be that as it may, correct English may soon be judged on the basis of international utility.
rather than on the ability to speak and pronounce the language like a native speaker.
The idea, however, is not new. Back in the 19th century, a famous writer said that so-called correct English was nothing but the slang of pedants!
We hope you will enjoy speaking English throughout the world and that your knowledge and understanding have been increased by studying this book.
